title: Conjecture 1.1
statement_text: Fix $p \in (0,1)$ and fix a graph $H$ with no isolated vertices. Then $$\max_{x \in \mathbb{N}} \Pr(X_H = x) = O\!\left(n^{1-v(H)}\right),$$ where $v(H)$ is the number of vertices of $H$.
context_text: The conjecture is motivated by the fact that $\operatorname{Var} X_H = \Theta(n^{2v(H)-2})$ for fixed $p$, so asymptotic normality implies $X_H$ is concentrated on an interval of length $\Theta(n^{v(H)-1})$; if the distribution is sufficiently smooth, each value in this interval should have comparable probability. The bound $n^{1-v(H)}$ would be best possible by Chebyshev's inequality. The assumption that $H$ has no isolated vertices is necessary, since removing them makes $X_H$ a deterministic multiple of the reduced count.
paper_contribution: Proves Theorem 1.2, an approximate version: $\max_{x \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pr(X_H = x) = n^{1-v(H)+o(1)}$ for every connected graph $H$ and fixed $p \in (0,1)$, matching the conjectured bound up to the $o(1)$ term in the exponent.
notes: PDF source. Conjecture attributed to the same authors via their prior paper [19]; the present paper proves an asymptotically tight approximate version for all connected H. The conjecture statement uses $x \in \mathbb{N}$ while Theorem 1.2 is stated for $x \in \mathbb{Z}$.
paper_title: Anticoncentration for subgraph counts in random graphs
